content: Just as the human brain leverages activated long-term memory synchronicity to retain critical information across long horizons of active learning, we can hypothesize that 2025 ICM curiosity-driven autonomous agents can offload frequent novel state representations to persistent 'activated long-term agent memory' rather than discarding them after short-term encoding. This retains the novelty signals for long-horizon exploration and avoids the common problem of catastrophic forgetting of previously discovered useful novel environments.
source_topics: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
trigger_topic: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
